title: The dignity of jobs that no one applauds
summary: Argue that workplace dignity is structural, not something handed down from balconies.
topic: The dignity of jobs that no one applauds
prompt: Write a short argumentative essay (200–280 words) about workplace dignity for jobs that are normally invisible (cleaning, refuse collection, care work, supermarket work). Argue what real recognition would actually look like. Use at least one passive construction and avoid empty conclusions.
hintStarters: During the pandemic, balconies all over Europe…
What troubles me about that arc is not that…
It is worth asking why we are so good at noticing…

hintVocab.meaningVi: trong khoảng cách đi bộ tới

sampleText:
During the pandemic, balconies all over Europe filled up at seven in the evening with the sound of clapping, and for a few weeks it seemed that society had finally remembered the people who hold it together. Nurses, supermarket workers, refuse collectors — they were named, photographed, and thanked. Then the clapping stopped, and so did most of the noticing.

What troubles me about that arc is not that the applause ended. Public emotion always ends. What troubles me is that we mistook the applause for recognition. A job is not recognised because it briefly trends; it is recognised when the people who do it are paid enough to rent within walking distance of where they work, when their bodies are not used up by the age of fifty, and when a child can say "my father empties bins" without the listener changing their face.

It is worth asking why we are so good at noticing the surgeon and so quick to forget the cleaner who keeps the operating room sterile. Part of it is that we mistake difficulty for importance. The cleaner's work is easy to learn; that does not make it easy to do, and it certainly does not make it dispensable.

Real dignity is structural. It is not something we hand down from a balcony. It is built into pay scales, schedules, and the small daily question of whether someone is allowed to be tired without being judged.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nhầm tràng vỗ tay với sự công nhận
trao xuống từ một ban công

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive voice for systemic claims
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'they were named, photographed, and thanked', 'a job is recognised', 'it is built into pay scales' đều dùng bị động để mô tả các hệ thống và quá trình.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'they were named, photographed, and thanked', 'a job is recognised', 'it is built into pay scales' all use the passive to describe systems and processes.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Parallel structure for emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'paid enough to rent…, not used up by fifty, and a child can say…' lặp cùng cấu trúc 'when …' để liệt kê các điều kiện của sự công nhận thật.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'paid enough to rent…, not used up by fifty, and a child can say…' repeats the 'when …' structure to list the conditions of real recognition.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong đại dịch, các ban công khắp châu Âu lấp đầy tiếng vỗ tay vào bảy giờ tối, và trong vài tuần, dường như xã hội cuối cùng đã nhớ đến những người giữ nó đứng vững. Y tá, nhân viên siêu thị, người thu gom rác — họ được gọi tên, được chụp ảnh, và được cảm ơn. Rồi tiếng vỗ tay ngưng, và sự để ý cũng ngưng theo.

Điều khiến tôi day dứt không phải là tiếng vỗ tay đã ngừng. Cảm xúc công chúng luôn ngừng. Điều khiến tôi day dứt là chúng ta đã nhầm tiếng vỗ tay với sự công nhận. Một công việc không được công nhận vì nó trở thành xu hướng trong thoáng chốc; nó được công nhận khi những người làm nó được trả đủ để thuê nhà trong khoảng cách đi bộ tới nơi làm việc, khi cơ thể họ không bị vắt kiệt trước năm mươi tuổi, và khi một đứa trẻ có thể nói "cha tôi đổ thùng rác" mà người nghe không đổi sắc mặt.

Thật đáng hỏi vì sao ta giỏi nhận ra bác sĩ phẫu thuật và lại nhanh chóng quên người lao công đang giữ phòng mổ vô trùng. Một phần vì ta nhầm độ khó với tầm quan trọng. Công việc của người lao công dễ học; điều đó không có nghĩa là nó dễ làm, và chắc chắn không có nghĩa là nó có thể bỏ đi.

Phẩm giá thực sự mang tính cấu trúc. Nó không phải là thứ ta trao xuống từ một ban công. Nó được xây dựng trong thang lương, lịch làm việc, và trong câu hỏi nhỏ mỗi ngày: liệu một người có được phép mệt mà không bị phán xét.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses three passive verbs in parallel correctly?
options: They were named, photographed, and thanked.
They are named and photograph and thanks.
They named, were photograph, and have thanks.
They had been name, photographs, and thank.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple passive applied three times in parallel: 'were named, photographed, and thanked'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for three past-participle forms after the same auxiliary.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the structure 'It is X who/that...' to focus attention?
options: What troubles me is that we mistook the applause for recognition.
Troubles me what we mistook applause recognition.
We troubled mistook applause recognition is.
It troubles, recognition, we mistook the applause.
mcQuestions.explanation: A 'What … is …' cleft delays and emphasises the key idea (here, that we confused applause for recognition).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a 'What … is …' or 'It is … who/that …' pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps parallel structure across a list of conditions?
options: When the people are paid enough, when their bodies are not used up by fifty, and when a child can say 'my father empties bins' without the listener changing their face.
When they paid enough, body used up not fifty, and a child saying father empties bins.
Pay enough, body fine, and child can speak the father empty bins.
If they have pay, no used up, and a child it can say.
mcQuestions.explanation: Three 'when …' clauses with parallel structure list the conditions cleanly.
mcQuestions.hint: All three list items should start with the same word.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option presents a sharp closing thought, not an empty conclusion?
options: In conclusion, we should all respect every job in society equally.
Real dignity is structural. It is not something we hand down from a balcony.
In today's fast-paced world, we tend to forget the people behind the work.
Throughout history, humans have always valued hard work above all.
mcQuestions.explanation: B uses a short, image-anchored claim — the kind of closing that lands. The others recycle banned filler.
mcQuestions.hint: The closing should give a sharp image, not a generic moral.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'mistake X for Y' correctly?
options: We mistook the applause for recognition.
We mistake applause that recognition.
We took applause mistake to recognition.
We have a mistake applause about recognition.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'mistake X for Y' is a fixed collocation; here past simple = 'mistook'.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the option that follows the 'mistake X for Y' pattern exactly.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you could change ONE structural thing about how an invisible job in your city is treated, what would it be, and why that one?
